question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user use the /health/readiness endpoint response timing to perform a binary search on database query latency, inferring database load and potentially the presence of other concurrent database operations, leaking information about other users' activity?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger an integer overflow or unexpected behavior by sending requests when the server port is configured as 0 (uint16 zero value), causing fmt.Sprintf('http://localhost:%d', port) to construct the URL 'http://localhost:0', which may resolve unexpectedly or cause the health check to always fail, enabling a persistent denial-of-service?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of request body size limits on GET /health/liveness or GET /health/readiness to send requests with extremely large bodies, consuming server memory and causing out-of-memory conditions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user cause information leakage through the log output by triggering repeated failures of the readiness check, causing log.Errorf to emit error messages containing internal server details (URLs, error codes, connection strings) that may be accessible through a log aggregation endpoint or misconfigured log exposure?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the DSN string constructed by rosettaConfig.Db.GetDsn() (containing host, port, username, dbname, password in plaintext) is passed to postgres.New() and potentially stored in memory accessible via a heap dump or memory disclosure vulnerability, leaking database credentials?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user use the /health/readiness endpoint to perform a denial-of-service against the PostgreSQL database by sending requests at a rate that causes the postgres health check to repeatedly open and close connections, triggering excessive authentication overhead on the database server?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of retry logic or circuit breaking in checkNetworkStatus() to cause cascading failures, where a temporarily unavailable internal network causes all concurrent GET /health/readiness requests to wait the full 10-second timeout before failing, blocking all health check goroutines simultaneously?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user send HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1 pipelined requests to /health/readiness to bypass any per-connection rate limiting, sending many health check requests over a single connection and amplifying the database connection exhaustion attack?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shared rosettaClient (created once via client.NewAPIClient(cfg)) to cause a connection pool starvation, where concurrent GET /health/readiness requests all compete for connections in the shared HTTP client, causing some requests to block indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user use the /health/readiness endpoint to detect database failover events in real time by monitoring the transition from 503 to 200 responses, enabling precise timing of attacks during the vulnerable window when the database is switching over?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the JSON response from GET /health/readiness to enumerate the exact names of internal health check components ('postgresql', 'network'), using this information to craft targeted attacks against those specific infrastructure components?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a resource leak by sending GET /health/readiness requests that are cancelled mid-flight (by closing the TCP connection), leaving the internal rosettaClient HTTP connections in a half-open state that are not properly cleaned up, gradually exhausting file descriptors?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user use the /health/liveness endpoint (which always returns 200 OK with no checks) to keep a load balancer from removing a completely broken server from rotation, since liveness always passes regardless of actual application health, causing traffic to continue flowing to a non-functional instance?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() uses a plain HTTP URL (http://localhost:{port}) rather than HTTPS, and if the server is misconfigured to listen on an external interface, cause the internal health check traffic to be intercepted or observed by other processes on the same host?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a denial of service by exploiting the sequential execution of both readiness checks (postgresql + network), where each check has a 10-second timeout, meaning each GET /health/readiness request can hold resources for up to 20 seconds in the worst case, enabling a low-rate attack with high resource impact?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of CORS restrictions on /health/liveness and /health/readiness to make cross-origin requests from a malicious web page, using the health endpoint responses to probe the server's internal state from a victim's browser?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a panic by sending GET /health/readiness when the internal NetworkList API returns a response where NetworkIdentifiers is a non-nil but zero-length slice, causing the unguarded networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] access to panic with 'index out of range [0] with length 0'?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user use the /health/readiness endpoint as a covert channel to infer whether a database migration or schema change is in progress, by observing the transition patterns of the 'postgresql' check between available and unavailable states?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the network health check calls /network/list and /network/status on the same server, creating a recursive dependency where the server's own health check contributes to its load, enabling a feedback loop attack where health check traffic degrades the server enough to fail the health check?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the postgres health check's behavior of opening a real database connection on every GET /health/readiness call to perform a credential brute-force oracle, where the check's success or failure reveals whether the configured database credentials are valid?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with specially crafted Accept headers to /health/readiness to probe for content negotiation vulnerabilities in the hellofresh/health-go/v4 HandlerFunc, potentially causing unexpected behavior or information disclosure in the response format?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a circuit breaker in checkNetworkStatus() to cause a thundering herd problem, where after a period of unavailability, many queued GET /health/readiness requests simultaneously attempt to reconnect to the internal network, overwhelming the server with a burst of internal API calls?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user use the /health/readiness endpoint to perform a slow-read attack, where the client reads the response very slowly, keeping the server's response writer open and consuming a goroutine for the duration, enabling a low-bandwidth denial-of-service?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the rosettaClient is initialized with client.NewConfiguration(serverUrl, 'readiness-check', nil) where the third parameter (nil) means no custom HTTP client is provided, relying on the default Go HTTP client with no timeout configured at the transport level, potentially allowing requests to hang indefinitely beyond the health check timeout?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: '] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the publicly exposed /health/readiness endpoint to perform reconnaissance on the database host and port by observing error messages in the JSON response when the PostgreSQL check fails with a connection refused error, revealing the database's network location?
